quote: poorly-written software, authored in part by GenAI, could spread malware leading to a massive security incident that dwarfs WannaCry
claim: GenAI-assisted code could lead to a massive security incident worse than WannaCry due to poor code quality.

quote: A $100B LLM is still an LLM. Driverless cars were crippled by the endless long tail of outliers; LLMs may well pattern similarly, working in many cases but often troubled by the unexpected, even when scaled.
claim: A $100B LLM will still suffer from the same fundamental limitations as current LLMs, particularly failure on outlier cases, regardless of scale.

quote: Reasoning and planning in unexpected scenarios are still likely to be flawed.
claim: LLM reasoning and planning in unexpected scenarios will remain flawed even at $100B scale.

quote: Even if a $100B LLM worked noticeably better than a $1B LLM, the economics for that investment are problematic.
claim: The economics of a $100B LLM are problematic because diminishing returns mean it would only be marginally better than far cheaper models on most tasks.

quote: Presumably the operating idea here is that (a) $100B will equal AGI and (b) that customers will pay literally anything for AGI. I doubt either premise is correct.
claim: Spending $100B on an LLM will not produce AGI, and customers would not pay unlimited fees for AGI even if it existed.

quote: If they are wrong, the net effect will be to delay AGI, because their project will siphon away talent from other approaches – just as it already has, but more so.
claim: The $100B Stargate project will delay AGI by siphoning talent away from alternative approaches to AI.

trigger: Wall Street Journal report on GenAI spending vs revenue
quote: $50B in, $3B out. That's obviously not sustainable.
claim: The generative AI industry spending $50B while generating only $3B in revenue is not a sustainable business model.

quote: If hallucinations were brought down to human expert levels by the end of 2024 I would be truly astonished. They are inherent to how the system makes predictions, and to the fact that LLMs themselves don't have the wherewithal to look things up and reason over them.
claim: LLM hallucinations will not be reduced to human expert levels by end of 2024 because they are inherent to how LLMs make predictions.

quote: The entire industry is based on hype, and on the specific hope that the kinds of problems we saw again and again with GPT-2, GPT-3, and GPT-4, not to mention Claude, Grok, Gemini, LLaMA, and so on, are on the verge of being solved — even as the reality is that all the big players are converging on systems that are roughly equivalent to GPT-4, all flawed in essentially the same ways.
claim: The GenAI industry is based on hype rather than substance, with all major models converging at GPT-4-level performance and sharing the same fundamental flaws.

quote: I am not confident Generative AI as currently envisioned will ever come together.
claim: Generative AI as currently envisioned may never achieve the reliability and capability needed to justify its costs.

quote: With everyone building similar models, there is no moat, hence lots of competition on price
claim: There is no competitive moat in LLMs because all major companies are building similar models, leading to price competition.

quote: current-sized models, already expensive, aren't reliable enough for many jobs
claim: Current LLMs are too unreliable for many production use cases while already being expensive to operate.

quote: So far most uses of LLMs have been for testing, not production-level systems.
claim: Most enterprise LLM deployments as of early 2024 are testing/experimental rather than production systems.

quote: the closest thing to a killer app is probably coding assistance, but the income there isn't massive enough to cover costs of the chips, legal etc.
claim: Coding assistance is the closest thing to a killer app for LLMs, but it does not generate enough revenue to cover infrastructure and legal costs.

quote: The initially-celebrated use case of "generative search" isn't reliable enough (and doesn't yet have a clear business model).
claim: Generative AI-powered search is not reliable enough for production use and lacks a viable business model.

quote: GPT-5 may be more satisfying but is likely even more expensive to train and operate.
claim: GPT-5 will likely be even more expensive to train and operate than GPT-4, compounding the profitability problem.

quote: If nobody (OpenAI, Google, or anyone else) releases a true quantum leap by the end of 2024, substantially addressing key issues around reliability, hallucination, data leakage, and security, the bubble may start to pop by this time next year.
claim: If no company releases a transformative AI model addressing reliability, hallucination, and security by end of 2024, the GenAI bubble may start popping by early 2025.

quote: GenAI will still exist, and find some use, but valuations and excitement may dissipate, reemerging only once genuine advances are made.
claim: Generative AI will persist but at reduced valuations and excitement levels until genuine technical advances are made.

quote: Nobody knows exactly how LLMs work, and that means that nobody can ever issue strong guarantees around them.
claim: It is impossible to provide strong security guarantees for LLMs because nobody fully understands how they work internally.

quote: Rule 1 of cybersecurity is to keep your attack surface small; in LLMs the attack surface appears to be infinite. That can't be good.
claim: LLMs have an effectively infinite attack surface, making them fundamentally insecure.

quote: Secure, trustworthy AI could be a great thing, but LLMs will never be that.
claim: LLMs will never be secure and trustworthy AI systems.

quote: If we don't get a handle on this problem, fast, nobody will believe anything.
claim: Without swift action against AI-generated misinformation, public trust in all information will collapse.

quote: fast, cheap, automated misinformation left unchecked threatens to undermine trust — and democracy itself.
claim: Unchecked AI-generated misinformation threatens to undermine both public trust and democratic institutions.

quote: we should require all AI-generated content to be labeled
claim: All AI-generated content should be required by law to be labeled as such.

trigger: New preprint on data and scaling (arXiv:2404.04125)
quote: Neural networks (at least in the configurations that have been dominant over the last three decades) have trouble generalizing beyond the multidimensional space that surrounds their training examples.
claim: Neural networks in their dominant configurations cannot reliably generalize beyond the space of their training examples.

trigger: New preprint on data and scaling (arXiv:2404.04125)
quote: That limits their ability to reason and plan reliably. It also drives their greediness with data
claim: Neural networks' inability to generalize beyond training data limits their reasoning and planning ability and drives their insatiable need for more data.

trigger: New preprint on data and scaling (arXiv:2404.04125)
quote: This is why driverless cars are still just demos, and why LLMs will never be reliable.
claim: LLMs will never be reliable because neural networks cannot generalize beyond their training distribution.

trigger: New preprint on data and scaling (arXiv:2404.04125)
quote: Sooner or later, the exponential greed for data will exceed what is available.
claim: The exponentially growing data requirements of LLMs will eventually exceed available training data.

trigger: New preprint on data and scaling (arXiv:2404.04125)
quote: To get to AGI, we need alternative approaches that can generalize better beyond the data on which they have been trained.
claim: Achieving AGI requires fundamentally different approaches from current neural networks that can generalize beyond training data.

trigger: NYT report on AI companies cutting ethical corners for data
quote: the brittleness (a term for unreliability); the greed for data that is causing the desperation that the Times described; the opacity (uninterpretability) that makes debugging, fairness, and engineering so hard; and the shallowness, meaning that generalizations are never complete.
claim: The four fundamental problems of deep learning identified in 2018 -- brittleness, data greed, opacity, and shallowness -- are exactly what is ailing GenAI in 2024.

trigger: NYT report on AI companies cutting ethical corners for data
quote: an intellectual monoculture — with no alternative approach that is nearly as well-funded
claim: AI research has become an intellectual monoculture focused on LLMs, with no alternative approach receiving comparable funding.

trigger: NYT report on AI companies cutting ethical corners for data
quote: a pile of unreliable systems that are unlikely to live up to the hype
claim: Current generative AI systems are unreliable and unlikely to live up to the hype surrounding them.

trigger: NYT report on AI companies cutting ethical corners for data
quote: We must invest heavily in alternative approaches and stop funding a losing hand to the infinite degree, crowding everything out.
claim: The AI field should invest heavily in alternative approaches to LLMs rather than continuing to pour resources into scaling current architectures.

trigger: Elon Musk AGI prediction on X Space
quote: I don't buy the predictions for the next year
claim: Musk's prediction that AI will be smarter than any individual human by end of 2025 is wrong.

quote: some late stage venture capital firms are sitting out the current GenAI rush
claim: Late-stage venture capital firms are beginning to avoid investing in the GenAI rush, signaling the bubble may not last.

quote: I could be persuaded that on some measures there was a doubling of capabilities for some set of months in 2020-2023, but I don't see that case at all for the last 13 months.
claim: LLM capabilities have not doubled in the 13 months since GPT-4's release, contrary to claims of continued exponential improvement.

quote: Instead, I see numerous signs that we have reached a period of diminishing returns.
claim: LLM development has entered a period of diminishing returns as of early 2024.

quote: Mollick's claim that "the rate of improvement in Large Language models show capabilities doubling every 5 to 14 months" is no longer holding true.
claim: The claim that LLM capabilities double every 5-14 months is no longer supported by recent evidence.

quote: The wall that I once warned about, in 2022, may finally be approaching.
claim: LLMs are approaching a capability wall or ceiling that limits further improvement.

quote: If we really have changed regimes, from rapid progress to diminishing returns, and hallucinations and stupid errors do linger, LLMs may never be ready for prime time.
claim: If LLM progress has shifted to diminishing returns, LLMs may never be reliable enough for production use.

quote: In the most extreme case, OpenAI's $86 billion valuation could look in hindsight like a WeWork moment for AI.
claim: OpenAI's $86 billion valuation may prove to be as overinflated as WeWork's was.

quote: If enthusiasm for GenAI dwindles and market valuations plummet, AI won't disappear, and LLMs won't disappear; they will still have their place as tools for statistical approximation.
claim: Even if the GenAI bubble bursts, LLMs will persist as useful tools for statistical approximation but at a lower scale than currently envisioned.

quote: Reliable, trustworthy AI is surely achievable, but we may need to go back to the drawing board to get there.
claim: Achieving reliable and trustworthy AI will require fundamentally new approaches beyond current LLM architectures.

quote: there about 5 - 7 recent models are on a par with GPT-4, but none is clearly and decisively ahead
claim: As of April 2024, 5-7 recent LLMs match GPT-4's level but none decisively surpasses it, suggesting convergence.

quote: Both damning and correct. People who believe in LLMs have played themselves.
claim: People who attribute deep intelligence to LLMs are being deceived by a mechanism analogous to a psychic's con -- the LLMentalist Effect.

quote: the cognitive capacities of LLMs are severely limited
claim: The cognitive capacities of LLMs are severely limited despite their impressive surface-level fluency.

quote: Right now there are likely hundreds, maybe thousands or even millions of such things.
claim: There are hundreds to millions of intellectual tasks that ordinary humans can do that AI cannot as of 2024.

quote: Reliably drive a car in a novel location that they haven't previously encountered, even in the face of unusual circumstances like hand-lettered signs, without the assistance of other humans.
claim: AI will not be able to reliably drive a car in novel locations with unusual circumstances by end of 2025.

quote: Learn and master the basics of almost any new video game within a few minutes or hours, and solve original puzzles in the alternate world of that video game.
claim: AI will not be able to master arbitrary new video games within minutes or hours by end of 2025.

quote: Write engaging biographies and obituaries without obvious hallucinations that aren't grounded in reliable sources.
claim: AI will not be able to write biographies and obituaries without hallucinations by end of 2025.

quote: Write cogent, persuasive legal briefs without hallucinating any cases
claim: AI will not be able to write legal briefs without hallucinating case citations by end of 2025.

quote: Write Pulitzer-caliber books, fiction and non-fiction
claim: AI will not be able to write Pulitzer-caliber books by end of 2025.

quote: Come up with paradigm-shifting, Nobel-caliber scientific discoveries.
claim: AI will not produce Nobel-caliber paradigm-shifting scientific discoveries by end of 2025.

quote: All of the above seem outside the bounds of current AI. None seem to me to be within immediate reach.
claim: A wide range of tasks from physical navigation to creative writing to scientific discovery remain outside the reach of current and near-term AI.

quote: they will not themselves, anytime soon, be virtual scientists
claim: LLMs will not function as virtual scientists capable of independent scientific discovery anytime soon.

quote: the multimodal stuff still is very buggy, having troubles with basics like the English alphabet
claim: Multimodal AI capabilities remain very buggy, struggling with basic tasks like rendering the English alphabet correctly.

quote: Yejin Choi worried that LLMs were not reliable. They still aren't. She was right.
claim: LLMs remain unreliable as of April 2024, confirming warnings from researchers like Yejin Choi.

quote: I warned that LLMs would be used by bad actors, and they increasingly are.
claim: LLMs are increasingly being used by bad actors for harmful purposes as predicted.

quote: hallucinations (e.g., on basic math) were again the key liability. You can't teach if your students don't know whether or not to believe you.
claim: LLM hallucinations are a fundamental barrier to using AI for education because students cannot trust unreliable outputs.

quote: I continue to have doubts about whether LLMs are really the right tool for the next step of the job
claim: LLMs may not be the right technology for advancing educational tools like Khan Academy.

quote: no company yet had A.I. technology that was sophisticated enough to make a virtual assistant answer questions reliably
claim: No company as of April 2024 has AI technology sophisticated enough to make a virtual assistant reliably answer questions.

quote: It's almost like a broken watch being right twice a day. It's right some of the time, but you don't know which part of the time, and that greatly diminishes its value.
claim: AI virtual assistants are unreliable in a way that greatly diminishes their value -- users cannot predict when they will be correct.

quote: I don't think we are any closer to a machine with the wherewithal or the motivation to annihilate the human species than we were a year ago.
claim: AI has not moved meaningfully closer to posing an existential risk to humanity between 2023 and 2024.

quote: Bugs in LLMs have stymied Khanmigo and the AI pin. Adding visual data to LLMs hasn't solved hallucinations.
claim: Adding multimodal capabilities to LLMs has not solved the hallucination problem.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: I don't see how we get to a positive AI future if we sweep all the problems under the rug.
claim: Achieving a positive AI future requires honestly confronting current AI problems rather than ignoring them.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: I think Khosla's timing (all this by 2049) might be off, perhaps by decades
claim: Vinod Khosla's predictions about AI transforming medicine, agriculture, and infrastructure by 2049 may be off by decades.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: current AI needs better spatial intelligence
claim: Current AI systems need fundamentally better spatial intelligence to achieve real-world physical capabilities.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: We won't get to a billion personal robots if they are as dodgy as driverless cars, frequently working, yet stymied often enough by outliers that we can't fully count on them.
claim: Achieving widespread personal robotics requires solving the reliability problem that plagues driverless cars -- robots that fail on outlier cases cannot be trusted.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: A billion robots with a superficial understanding of the physical world would be a recipe for disaster.
claim: Deploying robots at scale with only superficial physical world understanding would be dangerous.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: Dreamers who promise far more than they are likely to deliver are getting almost all the money, and (at least in AI) they are getting so much money for a path that it is unlikely to work that they are leaving too little oxygen for the exploration of new ideas that might work.
claim: Venture capital is disproportionately funding AI approaches that are unlikely to work, starving alternative approaches of resources.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: I doubt that anyone with a short leash can achieve the breakthroughs we will need, if we are to get to the optimistic future so many of us would like to see.
claim: Achieving AI breakthroughs requires long-term research funding, not the typical 3-year VC timeline for product-market fit.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: Cars would never have blossomed without streetlights and traffic laws, and too many people would have died without seatbelts.
claim: AI regulation is necessary for AI to flourish, just as traffic laws were necessary for automobiles to succeed.

trigger: TED 2024 talks by Suleyman, Khosla, Li
quote: We don't want AI to wind up stigmatized like nuclear energy, a good solution that was rolled out with insufficient care and permanently tarnished.
claim: Without proper regulation and care, AI risks becoming permanently stigmatized like nuclear energy.

quote: Like so many recent models, the new model approached GPT-4 but didn't exceed it, consistent (but not proof of) my ongoing conjecture of diminishing returns.
claim: Meta's LLAMA-3 approached but did not exceed GPT-4, providing further evidence for the hypothesis of diminishing returns in LLM scaling.

quote: Whatever tiny moat there was between the companies will further diminish. LLMs are well on their way to becoming a commodity; price wars will be intense.
claim: LLMs are becoming commoditized, which will erode competitive advantages and trigger intense price wars among providers.

trigger: Meta LLAMA-3 release and stock market decline
quote: 2024 could be remembered as the high point in a kind of tulip-mania.
claim: 2024 may mark the peak of a tulip-mania-like bubble in AI investment.

quote: all the major AI companies are spending billions producing almost exactly the same results using almost exactly the same data using almost exactly the same technology — all flawed in almost exactly the same ways
claim: All major AI companies are producing nearly identical LLM results with the same technology and data, all sharing the same fundamental flaws -- a dangerous groupthink.

quote: hallucinations are absolutely core to how LLMs work, and that we need new approaches based on different ideas.
claim: Hallucinations are a fundamental feature of how LLMs work, not a fixable bug, and solving the problem requires fundamentally new approaches.

quote: LLM "hallucinations" arise, regularly, because (a) they literally don't know the difference between truth and falsehood, (b) they don't have reliably reasoning processes to guarantee that their inferences are correct and (c) they are incapable of fact-checking their own work
claim: LLM hallucinations arise because LLMs cannot distinguish truth from falsehood, lack reliable reasoning processes, and cannot fact-check their own output

quote: everything that LLMs say -- true or false -- comes from the same process of statistically reconstructing what words are likely in some context. They NEVER fact-check what they say.
claim: All LLM outputs, whether true or false, come from the same statistical word-prediction process with no internal fact-checking mechanism

quote: even with perfect data, the stochastic reconstructive process would still produce some errors. The very process that LLMs use to generalize also creates hallucinations.
claim: Even with perfect training data, the stochastic generalization process used by LLMs would still produce hallucinations because the same mechanism that enables generalization also produces errors

quote: The field of AI will eventually do better, but probably with an AI that is structured differently, in which facts are first-class citizens, rather than something you hope you might get for free with enough data.
claim: Future AI systems that surpass current LLM limitations will likely need a different architecture where facts are first-class citizens rather than emergent from statistical patterns

quote: Silicon Valley is increasingly controlling our lives, and indeed the entire world, and that Washington (seat of the US government) is too much in their thrall to stand up tall enough against it
claim: Silicon Valley increasingly controls global affairs and the US government is too captured by tech industry influence to adequately regulate it

quote: What the tech industry wants, in their ideal world, is "self-regulation", in which they are pretty much free to do what they want. That's basically what's happened with social media, and it's been a disaster.
claim: Self-regulation of the tech industry, as occurred with social media, has been a disaster

quote: Particularly since Silicon Valley is prone to hype, Washington needs independent scientists in the room.
claim: Washington needs independent scientists involved in AI policy decisions because Silicon Valley is prone to hype

trigger: new White House AI advisory commission announcement
quote: basically it's regulatory capture all over again
claim: US government AI advisory commissions dominated by tech CEOs represent regulatory capture

quote: Washington remains in Silicon Valley's thrall, our elections are under siege, deepfake scams are everywhere, and not one law has passed.
claim: As of April 2024, no US federal AI legislation has passed despite growing threats from deepfakes and AI-enabled scams

trigger: Elon Musk announcement about X AI synthesis feature
quote: there is very little culture of fact checking there, and very little culture of trying to rationally reflect on and resolve competing opinions, which inevitably leads to extremism
claim: The platform X (Twitter) has very little culture of fact-checking or rational resolution of competing opinions, which leads to extremism

trigger: Elon Musk announcement about X AI synthesis feature
quote: LLMs are not up to that job. LLMs confined to X tweets will be even less grounded in reality.
claim: LLMs are not capable of reliable fact-checking and rational synthesis, and LLMs trained or confined to X/Twitter content will be even less grounded in reality

trigger: Elon Musk announcement about X AI synthesis feature
quote: Automatically amplifying the already inherent tendency towards extremism without fact-checking or reasoned analysis is a recipe for chaos.
claim: Using AI to automatically amplify social media content without fact-checking or reasoned analysis will amplify extremism and create information chaos

quote: Whether GenAI proves to be AGI is very much still up for grabs, and on any reasonable account we have a long way to go.
claim: Whether generative AI approaches can achieve AGI remains highly uncertain and there is still a long way to go

quote: For the last fourteen months we have seen no such dramatic improvement in capabilities—already evidence against his optimistic projection
claim: There has been no dramatic improvement in AI capabilities for fourteen months (since roughly March 2023), which is evidence against claims of continuous exponential progress

quote: there are increasing concerns about whether there is enough high-quality data to sustain prior improvements, as well as in-principle concerns re hallucinations, reasoning, planning, outliers and reliability
claim: There are increasing concerns that there may not be enough high-quality training data to sustain the rate of AI improvement seen previously

quote: knowing how to build better (or at least bigger) GenAI doesn't necessarily get us to AGI
claim: Building larger generative AI models does not necessarily lead to AGI; the assertion that AGI is simply a function of cash spend is a logical fallacy

quote: In the last year, his company has released no major update to GPT-4, and many competitors have caught up or are close.
claim: As of May 2024, OpenAI has released no major update to GPT-4 in a year, and competitors have caught up or are close to catching up

quote: Meta will likely release a GPT-4 level model within months – for free. If OpenAI has a moat (beyond initial customers, which could potentially move), we haven't seen it.
claim: Meta will likely release a GPT-4-level model for free within months, and OpenAI has no visible competitive moat beyond its existing customer base

quote: The company has not demo'd a working, reliable agent, and does not seem to have GPT-5 level model thus far.
claim: As of May 2024, OpenAI has not demonstrated a working reliable AI agent and does not appear to have a GPT-5-level model

quote: In seeking to grab vast resources for an approach that is deeply flawed, Altman is diverting resources from alternative approaches from AI that might be more trustworthy, reliable, and interpretable, potentially incurring considerable environment costs
claim: The massive investment in generative AI diverts resources from alternative AI approaches that might be more trustworthy, reliable, and interpretable, while also incurring significant environmental costs

trigger: reports of Microsoft building internal GPT-4 competitor
quote: Satya isn't fooling. Breaking news, this is just in, from The Information. Playing the field with Mistral wasn't enough; now he is building a really big model, big enough to compete directly with GPT-4, internally
claim: Microsoft is building an internal AI model large enough to compete directly with GPT-4, indicating possible loss of confidence in OpenAI

quote: If OpenAI had GPT-5, they have would shown it.
claim: OpenAI's release of GPT-4o rather than GPT-5 indicates they do not have a GPT-5-level model ready

quote: They don't have GPT-5 after 14 months of trying.
claim: OpenAI has failed to produce a GPT-5-level model after 14 months of effort

quote: the most important thing about the figure is that 4o is not a lot different from Turbo, which is not hugely different from 4
claim: GPT-4o represents only marginal improvement over GPT-4 Turbo, which itself was not hugely different from GPT-4

quote: OpenAI has presumably pivoted to new features precisely because they don't know how produce the kind of capability advance that the "exponential improvement" would have predicted.
claim: OpenAI pivoted to adding new features (like speech) rather than core capability improvements because they cannot produce the capability advances that exponential improvement narratives would predict

quote: each day in which there is no GPT-5 level model–from OpenAI or any of their well-financed, well-motivated competitors—is evidence that we may have reached a phase of diminishing returns
claim: The continued absence of a GPT-5-level model from any well-funded AI lab is evidence that the field may have reached a phase of diminishing returns

trigger: Ilya Sutskever and Jan Leike departures from OpenAI
quote: I suspects it is about an attitude: the company's attitude towards AI safety.
claim: Key safety researchers left OpenAI because of the company's deteriorating attitude towards AI safety, not because of a specific scary algorithm

trigger: Ilya Sutskever and Jan Leike departures from OpenAI
quote: People don't leave companies with $86 billion valuations for nothing. Four key resignations thus far in 2024 is not a good sign.
claim: Four key AI safety resignations from OpenAI in 2024 is a significant negative signal about the company's safety culture

trigger: Ilya Sutskever and Jan Leike departures from OpenAI
quote: Is OpenAI's push to commercialization coming at the expense of AI safety?
claim: OpenAI's push toward commercialization is coming at the expense of its AI safety work

quote: AI didn't *have* to go down such a shitty path of enabling and accelerating surveillance capitalism, misinformation, nonconsensual deepfake porn, IP theft, etc.
claim: AI development has gone down a harmful path of enabling surveillance capitalism, misinformation, nonconsensual deepfake porn, and IP theft, when it did not have to

quote: the science has been corrupted by money
claim: AI science has been corrupted by commercial financial incentives

quote: the claim that the resemblance to ScarJo was a "coincidence" was a lie. Sam knew perfectly well what the character sounded like.
claim: OpenAI's claim that the GPT-4o voice's resemblance to Scarlett Johansson was coincidental was a deliberate lie

quote: Spin is a way of life at OpenAI; telling the truth is not.
claim: OpenAI as an organization has a systemic pattern of spin and dishonesty

quote: I saw that with his fudges to the Senate about OpenAI equity (he has indirect equity, which he failed to mention, and owns an OpenAI VC firm that trades on the company name that he failed to mention), the board saw it with his lies about Helen Toner, and now we all see it with his embarrassing lies about Scarlett Johansson. It's a pattern.
claim: Sam Altman has a pattern of dishonesty including misleading Senate testimony about his equity, lying about Helen Toner, and lying about the Scarlett Johansson voice

trigger: Google AI Overview pizza glue error
quote: the regurgitative process need not be verbatim
claim: LLMs can regurgitate training data in non-verbatim forms through partial regurgitation, paraphrasing source material while adding little conceptual content

trigger: Google AI Overview pizza glue error
quote: By clustering piles of similar things together in a giant n-dimensional space, they pretty much automatically become synonym and pastische machines, regurgitating a lot of words with slight paraphrases while adding conceptually little, and understanding even less.
claim: LLMs function as synonym and pastiche machines that regurgitate training data with slight paraphrases while adding little conceptual content and having minimal genuine understanding

trigger: Google AI Overview pizza glue error
quote: Partial regurgitation, no matter how fluent, does not, and will not ever, constitute genuine comprehension.
claim: Partial regurgitation by LLMs, regardless of fluency, does not and will never constitute genuine comprehension

trigger: Google AI Overview pizza glue error
quote: Getting to real AI will require a different approach.
claim: Achieving real AI (with genuine understanding) will require a fundamentally different approach than current LLM architectures

quote: OpenAI apparently hasn't even fulfilled their own promises to devote 20% resources to AI safety
claim: OpenAI has not fulfilled its promise to devote 20% of its resources to AI safety

quote: By comparison, GoogleDeepMind devotes a lot of its energy towards projects like AlphaFold that have clear potential to help humanity. I don't see OpenAI doing much of that.
claim: GoogleDeepMind devotes significant energy to projects with clear humanitarian potential like AlphaFold, while OpenAI does not comparably invest in such work

quote: The real issue isn't whether OpenAI would win in court, it's what happens to all of us, if a company with a track record for cutting ethical corners winds up first to AGI.
claim: OpenAI has a track record of cutting ethical corners, which poses a serious risk if they are first to achieve AGI

quote: it shows a significant gap between the rosy rhetoric and actual reality, and in my view that gap has only grown over time, particularly over the last year or so
claim: There is a significant and growing gap between OpenAI's public rhetoric about benefiting humanity and its actual behavior

quote: I think it is correct for the public to take everything OpenAI says with a grain of salt. But OpenAI still have massive power, and with it a chance to screw up, and possibly put humanity at risk. If they ever got to AGI, I would be worried.
claim: The public should be skeptical of everything OpenAI says, and if OpenAI achieved AGI given its track record, that would be worrying for humanity

quote: Just because someone is rich or successful doesn't necessarily mean they should be trusted.
claim: Wealth and success in tech should not be treated as proxies for trustworthiness in AI governance

quote: Without independent scientists in the loop, with a real voice, we are lost.
claim: Independent scientists must have a real voice in AI governance; without them, effective oversight is impossible

quote: our only real hope is if citizens speak up
claim: Citizen engagement is the only real hope for achieving adequate AI governance, since governments are too captured by industry

trigger: Musk vs LeCun public argument on X
quote: the chatbots of both X.ai and Meta still seem to me to be wildly inadequate, hallucinatory machines that merely hint at what AI could be, but with precious little understanding of the concepts underlying the words they so fluently use
claim: Both Grok (X.ai) and Meta's AI chatbots are wildly inadequate and hallucinatory, with very little understanding of the concepts underlying their fluent language

trigger: Musk vs LeCun public argument on X
quote: Both Musk and LeCun (via Meta) command massive budgets to explore AI; I am not convinced that either is making real progress. Musk is just building bigger and bigger LLMs, and Meta is mostly (despite LeCun's reservations) mostly doing the same.
claim: Neither Musk/X.ai nor LeCun/Meta is making real progress toward AI breakthroughs despite massive budgets; both are mostly just building bigger LLMs
target: AI progress at X.ai and Meta

trigger: Musk vs LeCun public argument on X
quote: we need more science if we are to get to AGI. Right now we have a lot of engineering and alchemy, but not enough basic principles and formal understanding.
claim: Current AI development involves too much engineering and alchemy and not enough basic science; more fundamental scientific understanding is needed to reach AGI

trigger: Helen Toner TED AI podcast interview
quote: For years, Sam had made it really difficult for the board to do that job [of following the non-profit mission for humanity] by, you know, withholding information, misrepresenting things that were happening at the company, in some cases outright lying to the board.
claim: Sam Altman systematically withheld information, misrepresented company events, and outright lied to the OpenAI board for years, as confirmed by former board member Helen Toner

trigger: Helen Toner TED AI podcast interview
quote: When the board said Sam was "not consistently candid", they meant, shockingly, that Sam was not consistently candid.
claim: The original OpenAI board's stated reason for firing Altman -- that he was not consistently candid -- was literally true and substantiated

trigger: Helen Toner TED AI podcast interview
quote: we can't have the CEO of a company that potentially powerful behaving that way
claim: The CEO of a company as potentially powerful as OpenAI cannot be allowed to behave with the level of dishonesty Altman has demonstrated

trigger: OpenAI board (Taylor and Summers) Economist op-ed defending Altman
quote: the essay attacks a strawman, viz. that the firing of Altman was somehow precipitated by an immediate safety concern), thereby diverting from the board's actual, stated concern (candor)
claim: The new OpenAI board's defense of Altman in The Economist was a strawman argument that substituted safety concerns for the actual issue of candor/trust

trigger: OpenAI board (Taylor and Summers) Economist op-ed defending Altman
quote: if Altman can't be trusted, safety could become a serious issue
claim: If Altman cannot be trusted, OpenAI's AI safety could become a serious issue because trustworthy leadership is a prerequisite for safe AI development

trigger: OpenAI board (Taylor and Summers) Economist op-ed defending Altman
quote: The failure of two members of the new board (include Taylor, who chairs that Board) to draw that connection, coupled with their misdirection and unfair attack on Toner, is absolutely terrifying.
claim: The new OpenAI board's failure to recognize that Altman's trustworthiness issues are relevant to AI safety, combined with their attack on whistleblower Toner, indicates the board cannot fulfill its safety oversight role

trigger: OpenAI board (Taylor and Summers) Economist op-ed defending Altman
quote: If OpenAI does eventually develop deeply risky technology, it is incumbent on the nonprofit's board to address that, and if they can't trust Altman, I don't see they can do their job.
claim: The OpenAI nonprofit board cannot fulfill its safety oversight mission if it cannot trust Altman, which makes it unable to address deeply risky technology

trigger: OpenAI board (Taylor and Summers) Economist op-ed defending Altman
quote: OpenAI does not behave like a nonprofit and has not for a long time. If they cannot assemble a board that respects the legal filings they made, and cannot behave in keeping with their oft-repeated promises, they must dissolve the nonprofit
claim: OpenAI has not behaved like a nonprofit for a long time and should be required to dissolve its nonprofit status if it cannot assemble a board that respects its legal filings and stated mission

trigger: Elon Musk reposting Joscha Bach meme about Marcus
quote: reliability is still a dream, and of course toxicity and hallucinations are still with us. Worse, we are no closer to a solution to the alignment problem now than we were then.
claim: As of June 2024, AI reliability remains unsolved, toxicity and hallucinations persist, and the field is no closer to solving the alignment problem than it was 26 months prior

trigger: Elon Musk reposting Joscha Bach meme about Marcus
quote: For all the daily claims of "exponential progress", and for all the tens of billions that have been invested since, reliability is still a dream
claim: Despite tens of billions of dollars invested and claims of exponential progress, AI reliability remains unachieved

trigger: multiple AI industry events in late May 2024
quote: it has been almost two years since there's been a bona fide GPT-4-sized breakthrough, despite the constant boasts of exponential progress, with no real solution to hallucinations, unreliability, poor reasoning or shaky alignment— even though the field has invested more than $50B in efforts to power beyond GPT-4
claim: As of June 2024, there has been no GPT-4-level breakthrough in almost two years despite over $50 billion invested, and hallucinations, unreliability, poor reasoning, and alignment remain unsolved

quote: The pace of innovation in AI is slowing, its usefulness is limited, and the cost of running it remains exorbitant.
claim: The pace of AI innovation is slowing, its practical usefulness is limited, and operational costs remain exorbitant

quote: deep learning as we knew it then/as it was mostly commonly used then was greedy, heavily dependent on massive data, and that it would have trouble reasoning
claim: Deep learning is data-greedy, heavily dependent on massive data, and has trouble with reasoning

quote: LLMs were subject to essentially the same critique: greedy with respect to data, uninterpretable, unreliable as reasoners, subject to hallucinations
claim: LLMs are data-greedy, uninterpretable, unreliable as reasoners, and subject to hallucinations

quote: they lacked world models
claim: LLMs lack world models

quote: they don't develop robust representations of *how events unfold over time*
claim: LLMs do not develop robust representations of how events unfold over time

quote: they would be inadequate on their own
claim: LLMs are inadequate as standalone systems for achieving general intelligence

quote: scale was not all we need
claim: Scaling alone is not sufficient for achieving general AI capabilities

quote: the importance of representing kinds distinctly from individuals, separating reasoning from latent representations, incorporating explicit compositional structure and explicit operations over variables, incorporating some degree of innate pre learning, and exploiting explicit knowledge databases
claim: AI systems need to represent kinds distinctly from individuals, separate reasoning from latent representations, incorporate explicit compositional structure and operations over variables, incorporate innate priors, and exploit explicit knowledge databases

quote: DeepMind, among others, has achieved some strong results with neurosymbolic approaches (e.g., AlphaGeometry)
claim: Neurosymbolic approaches have achieved strong results, as demonstrated by DeepMind's AlphaGeometry

quote: models need to innately be more structured than pure multilayer perceptions, even prior to learning
claim: AI models need innate structure beyond pure multilayer perceptrons, prior to learning

quote: GPT-4 is not actually equivalent to a smart high schooler. It can do some things better, some worse, very few reliably.
claim: GPT-4 is not equivalent to a smart high schooler; it performs some tasks better, some worse, and very few reliably

quote: I would not hire it to do most of things that high school students are often hired to do, like work as a lifeguard, work in restaurants, work in a grocery store
claim: GPT-4 could not perform most jobs that high school students are hired to do, such as lifeguarding, restaurant work, or grocery store work

quote: The graph assumes that all continued progress will be same — ignoring qualitatively unsolved problems (hallucinations, planning, reasoning etc), the fact that synthetic data may not be as useful as natural data, limits on then-possible energy distribution and compute
claim: Projections of continued AI progress at past rates ignore qualitatively unsolved problems like hallucinations, planning, and reasoning, the limited utility of synthetic data compared to natural data, and energy and compute limits

quote: GPT-4 existed in August 2022. I can't think of a single measure in which any existing model in 2024 outpace GPT-4 by as much as GPT-4 outpaced the state of the art in 2020. We are already behind the curve.
claim: As of mid-2024, no existing model outpaces GPT-4 by as much as GPT-4 outpaced the 2020 state of the art, indicating AI progress is already behind exponential projections

quote: self-regulation is a farce
claim: Self-regulation by AI companies is ineffective

quote: the US legislature has made almost no progress thus far in reining in Silicon Valley
claim: The US legislature has made almost no progress in regulating Silicon Valley as of mid-2024

trigger: Pitchbook report on GenAI seed funding drop
quote: First sign of GenAI winter?
claim: Declining GenAI seed-stage funding is an early indicator of a potential GenAI winter

trigger: California SB-1047 AI safety bill backlash
quote: We should be making SB-1047 stronger, not weaker.
claim: California's SB-1047 AI safety bill should be strengthened, not weakened

trigger: California SB-1047 AI safety bill backlash
quote: Fine to talk big in front of the Senate, but when push comes to shove, nobody in the industry seems to actually want the kind of regulation they previously hinted at.
claim: AI companies publicly support regulation but oppose it when actual legislation is proposed

trigger: California SB-1047 AI safety bill backlash
quote: Big tech seemingly will not be content until they have squeezed out every last cent of profit, whilst socializing the costs of every last risk.
claim: Big tech companies seek to maximize profits while externalizing AI risks to society

trigger: California SB-1047 AI safety bill backlash
quote: An unregulated AI industry is a recipe for disaster.
claim: An unregulated AI industry will lead to disaster

quote: the database that indexes the whole thing is in plain text (except on systems that are explicitly set to encrypt everything). That's bananas. Keystone Kops level security.
claim: Microsoft Recall's plain-text database demonstrates that tech companies cannot be trusted to self-regulate on security and privacy

trigger: California SB-1047 AI safety bill backlash
quote: AI companies should be subject to liability and basic safety requirements, just as companies in other industries are
claim: AI companies should be subject to liability and basic safety requirements comparable to other industries

quote: As of today, I am more confident than ever that GPT-5 won't land this year.
claim: GPT-5 will not be released in 2024

quote: GPT-5 will continue to hallucinate and make a bunch of wacky errors, whenever it finally drops
claim: GPT-5, whenever released, will still hallucinate and make significant errors

quote: with GPT 4-Turbo and 4o perhaps being failed attempts that didn't seem worthy of the name, with another earlier attempt halting before Sam's time speaking to the Senate in May 2023
claim: GPT-4 Turbo and GPT-4o may have been failed attempts at GPT-5 that didn't meet the bar for the GPT-5 name

quote: stands by his own prediction that we will not see AGI by 2029
claim: AGI will not be achieved by 2029

quote: LLMs are not AGI, and (on their own) never will be; scaling alone was never going to be enough.
claim: LLMs are not AGI and will never achieve AGI on their own; scaling alone is insufficient

quote: The only mystery was what would happen when the big players realized that the jig was up, and that scaling was not in fact "All You Need".
claim: Major AI companies are beginning to realize that scaling LLMs is insufficient for achieving AGI

quote: inside the labs we have these capable models and they're not that far ahead
claim: OpenAI's CTO admitted there is no mind-blowing GPT-5 behind the scenes as of mid-2024

quote: Expect more revisionism and downsized expectations throughout 2024 and 2025.
claim: AI industry leaders will increasingly revise their timelines downward and reduce expectations throughout 2024 and 2025

quote: Generative AI does in fact (still) have enormous limitations
claim: Generative AI still has enormous limitations as of mid-2024

quote: It still hallucinates, it still makes boneheaded mistakes, and the whole Generative AI thing is looking more and more like a dud
claim: Generative AI continues to hallucinate and make significant errors, and is increasingly looking like a commercial dud

quote: OpenAI may in fact yet turn out to be the WeWork of AI
claim: OpenAI may turn out to be the WeWork of AI -- a massively overvalued company that fails to deliver

quote: Scaling is not enough. Returns are starting to diminish. We do need new approaches to AI.
claim: Returns from scaling AI are diminishing and new approaches to AI are needed

quote: AI is going to change everything, if not tomorrow, sometime over the next 5-20 years, some ways for good, some for bad
claim: AI will change everything over the next 5-20 years, in both positive and negative ways

quote: The markers that our next President puts down—about how much liability and responsibility companies will have, and about how the government keeps up with a rapidly changing technology— will have lasting impact.
claim: US presidential AI policy decisions in the near term will have lasting impact on liability, responsibility, and government capacity to keep up with AI

quote: Altman, for example, promised a week ago at Aspen Ideas that AI would "discover all of physics", a claim that is neither coherent nor plausible anytime in the next couple decades
claim: The claim that AI will discover all of physics is neither coherent nor plausible within the next couple of decades

quote: We need a President who can recognize when corporate leaders are promising things far beyond is currently realistic, who can sort truth from bullshit, in order to develop AI policies that are grounded in reality.
claim: The US needs a president who can distinguish AI hype from reality and develop grounded AI policy

quote: we need a President who can get Congress (which has this far dithered) to recognize the true urgency of the moment, since Executive Orders alone are not enough
claim: Executive orders alone are insufficient for AI governance; Congressional legislation is needed

trigger: Bill Gates clip on AI scaling
quote: so many folks have been brainwashed by the "scale is all you need" notion and "AGI in 2025" (or 2027 or 2029) nonsense, none of which seems remotely plausible to me as a cognitive scientist
claim: The notion that scaling alone will achieve AGI by 2025, 2027, or 2029 is not remotely plausible

trigger: Bill Gates clip on AI scaling
quote: many tech leaders have discovered that the best way to raise valuations is to hint that AGI is imminent, always on the dubious premise that scaling will keep going indefinitely; it behooves them financially to pretend
claim: Tech leaders hint at imminent AGI to raise valuations, driven by financial incentives rather than technical reality

trigger: Bill Gates clip on AI scaling
quote: we need techniques, such as metacognition, viz. systems that can reflect on what is needed and how to achieve it, "understanding how to think about a problem in a broad sense and be able to step back" and reflect
claim: AI progress requires metacognition -- systems that can reflect on problems and evaluate their own reasoning approaches

trigger: Bill Gates clip on AI scaling
quote: Spending upwards of 100 billion dollars on the current approach, which is what two more iterations of scaling would likely require, given that is unlikely to get to AGI (or ever be reliable) seems wasteful to me
claim: Spending $100+ billion on further LLM scaling is wasteful because it is unlikely to achieve AGI or reliable AI

trigger: Bill Gates clip on AI scaling
quote: I don't think metacognition can work without bringing explicit symbols (the basis of classical AI) back into the mix
claim: Metacognition in AI requires explicit symbolic representations and cannot work with neural networks alone

trigger: Bill Gates clip on AI scaling
quote: Neurosymbolic AI has long been an underdog; in the end I expect it to come from behind and be essential.
claim: Neurosymbolic AI will eventually prove essential to achieving advanced AI

quote: If a single bug can take down airlines, banks, retailers, media outlets, and more, what on earth makes you think we are ready for AGI?
claim: The fragility of current software infrastructure, as demonstrated by the CrowdStrike outage, shows the world is not ready for AGI

quote: The world needs to up its software game massively. We need to invest in improving software reliability and methodology, not rushing out half-baked chatbots.
claim: The world needs to invest in improving software reliability and methodology rather than rushing out AI chatbots

quote: part of the reason we are struggling with AI in complex AI systems is that we still lack adequate techniques for engineering complex systems
claim: AI struggles are partly due to the field still lacking adequate techniques for engineering complex software systems

quote: Chasing black box AI, difficult to interpret, and difficult to debug, is not the answer.
claim: Pursuing black-box AI that is difficult to interpret and debug is not the path to reliable AI

quote: leaving more and more code writing to generative AI, which grasps syntax but not meaning, as a new benchmark from Stephen Wolfram shows, is not the answer, either
claim: Generative AI grasps code syntax but not meaning, making it inadequate as a replacement for human code writing

quote: An unregulated AI industry is a recipe for disaster.
claim: An unregulated AI industry will lead to disaster

quote: Long-term adoption (vs mere experimentation) has been modest, there is no significantly better tech now than there was in January; GPT-5 is still a no show.
claim: Long-term enterprise adoption of GenAI has been modest rather than transformative, with no significant technical improvement from January to July 2024

quote: LLMs have just became exactly the commodity I predicted they would become, at the lowest possible price. How can OpenAI survive in that context?
claim: LLMs have become commoditized, with Meta offering GPT-4-level models for free, undermining OpenAI's business model

quote: OpenAI, as far as I can tell, doesn't really have any moat whatsoever, beyond brand recognition
claim: OpenAI has no meaningful competitive moat beyond brand recognition

quote: OpenAI could wind up being seen as the WeWork of AI
claim: OpenAI could end up being seen as the WeWork of AI due to huge losses, no moat, and commoditized technology

quote: The neural network approach known as generative AI — especially its poster chatbot ChatGPT – has captured the world's imagination, but will soon flame out.
claim: Generative AI will soon flame out in terms of public enthusiasm and commercial viability

quote: I fully expect that the generative AI bubble will begin to burst within the next 12 months
claim: The generative AI financial bubble will begin to burst within 12 months of July 2024

quote: The current approach has reached a plateau
claim: LLM capabilities have reached a plateau as of mid-2024

quote: There is no killer app
claim: There is no killer app for generative AI

quote: Nobody has a moat
claim: No GenAI company has a meaningful competitive moat

quote: I am certain that the impending collapse won't lead to the absolute death of AI. There is too much at stake.
claim: The collapse of the GenAI bubble will not lead to the death of AI as a field
